mapping.a_element: stability condition of critical points in moduli potential (dependent on uplifting amount)
mapping.b_element: probability of large deviations of eigenvalues (dependent on barrier positions ζ₁, ζ₂)

mapping.a_element: supersymmetric critical points (AdS maxima or minima of the Kähler function)
mapping.b_element: eigenvalue distributions constrained by barriers [ζ₁, ζ₂]

mapping.a_element: parameter space region where critical points remain stable for any uplifting
mapping.b_element: universal exponent θ(0) governing the decay of the probability that all eigenvalues lie in a fixed interval

mapping.a_element: displacement of potential minimum due to uplifting
mapping.b_element: shift in eigenvalue density due to barrier constraints (inverse square-root singularity)

brief_justification: Paper A analyzes stability of critical points in a moduli potential under F-term uplifting using supersymmetry and Kähler invariance, while Paper B studies extreme value statistics of eigenvalues in Gaussian random matrices via large deviation theory and barrier constraints. The shared language of 'stability' and 'critical points' masks fundamentally different mechanisms: one is a dynamical system in string theory, the other a probabilistic ensemble in random matrix theory.
